_meta.description: Artist style templates for Scales.sin chord suggestion engine. Fetched at runtime by the PWA.

7.label: Dominant b9 — tritone tension (omit 5)
7.source: D'Angelo — Brown Sugar (F7 tritone sub for B7)

Maj7.label: Maj7 #11 — Lydian sound (omit 5, tritone on top)
Maj7.source: Frank Ocean — White Ferrari / Ivy (Lydian mode center)

m7.label: Minor 13 — omit root + 5, keep b3 (distinguishes from sus4)
m7.source: Leon Thomas / Herbie Hancock jazz-funk tradition

m7.label: Minor 9 — shell voicing (b3, b7, 9) — Leon Thomas signature
m7.source: Leon Thomas — MUTT (Fm7 center), modal neo-soul

m7.label: Minor 11 — omit root + 5, keep b3 (modal float)

frank_ocean.name: Frank Ocean
frank_ocean.description: Mixolydian bVII borrow as signature move. Modal mixture as atmosphere, not modulation. Avoids dominant resolution — sus chords or modal color replace V7. Sparse harmonic rhythm on Blonde; denser jazz vocab on Channel Orange.

progressions.label: Nikes — Mixolydian bVII borrow
progressions.notes: Dbmaj7–Cm–Eb–Gm in Eb. Flat-VII (Dbmaj7) is Frank's most repeated harmonic move — Mixolydian borrow in a minor context.

progressions.label: Thinkin Bout You — major diatonic verse
progressions.notes: Fmaj7–Dm7–Em7–Am in C major. Textbook diatonic but voiced with maj7 extensions throughout.

progressions.label: Super Rich Kids — jazz altered dominant
progressions.notes: Ebmaj9–Ab13sus4–Dbmaj9–Bb9sus4–Bb7#5b9. Most harmonically advanced track — jazz altered dominant at the turnaround. Hooktheory novelty: 98/100.

progressions.label: Pyramids — minor vamp, modal ambiguity
progressions.notes: Fm–Cm–Ab–Gm in F minor. Aeolian/Dorian ambiguity, never raises 7th. Leon Thomas / D'Angelo shared vocabulary.

progressions.label: Pink + White — four-mode simultaneous mixture
progressions.notes: Cmaj7–Bm7–Amaj7 in A Dorian. Simultaneous modal mixture across 4 modes — most academically documented Frank Ocean progression.

progressions.label: Siegfried — Lydian II major borrow
progressions.notes: Cmaj7–Am9–Em–Dmaj7 in C. Dmaj7 is Lydian-inflected borrow (IImaj7) — unresolved, floating.

progressions.label: Solo — gospel plagal cadence
progressions.notes: Eb–Ab–Eb–Fm. IV-I plagal cadence, direct gospel reference. Same move as Kirk Franklin / Fred Hammond.

rules: bVII major chord (Mixolydian borrow) is the primary color move — use in both major and minor contexts
Avoid raised 7th in dominant context — use 9sus4 or 13sus4 instead of V7
Modal mixture as atmosphere: borrow from parallel modes without announcing it
Sparse harmonic rhythm — hold chords 2x longer than feels right
Extended chords (maj7, min9, maj9) as default voicing — triads only for contrast
Dorian as default minor (raised 6th, no leading tone) — shared with D'Angelo and Leon Thomas
Lydian IImaj7 or bVIImaj7 for unresolved, floating tonic feel

dangelo.name: D'Angelo
dangelo.description: Modal-funk loops rooted in Mixolydian and Dorian vamps where extensions live in the bass and vocal stacks rather than the keyboard voicing, and tritone substitutions and rhythmic displacement do the harmonic heavy lifting.

progressions.label: Untitled (How Does It Feel) — D Mixolydian main vamp
progressions.notes: Dadd9 – A7sus4 – G6 – C9. C9 (bVII9) is the modal indicator signaling D Mixolydian. A7sus4 holds dominant tension without resolving. Freestyle — exact voicings vary per performance.

progressions.label: Untitled (How Does It Feel) — pre-chorus shift
progressions.notes: F#m7 – B7 – Em9 – Asus. Momentary functional motion inside a mostly modal framework.

progressions.label: Brown Sugar — G Mixolydian tritone sub
progressions.notes: Em7 – A7 – Bm7 – F7. F7 is a tritone substitution for B7 (both share tritone D#/A). Vocal recast from C major into G Mixolydian. Classic D'Angelo harmonic sophistication move.

progressions.label: Left & Right — C minor neo-soul minor
progressions.notes: Cm7 – Fm7 – Ab – G7. Ab (bVI) borrowed from parallel major — classic neo-soul color move. Arranged in F Major but functions in C minor.

progressions.label: Send It On — F major iii-ii-Vsus-I loop
progressions.notes: Am7 – Gm7 – C7sus – Fmaj7. C7sus replaces tritone dominant, leaving harmonic space open. 6/8 swing feel at 127 BPM. Horn arrangement fills harmonic density above sparse keyboard.

progressions.label: Chicken Grease — A major one-chord dominant vamp
progressions.notes: A9 dominant 9th chord comped in 16th-note stabs. Harmony is the rhythm — James Brown philosophy. Groove subdivides the bar; chord stays static.

rules: Default to Mixolydian (bVII as modal color) and Dorian (minor with raised 6th on IV) — avoid committing to a single mode.
Do NOT resolve to V7→I. The bVII chord is a color and rest point, not a backdoor dominant.
Extensions (9ths, 11ths) are implied by bass and vocal stacks — keyboard uses shell voicings (root + 3rd + 7th, omit 5th).
Sus chords (A7sus4, C7sus) are resting points, not tension before resolution.
Tritone substitutions are reserved for specific moments (V of ii area) — not a general tool.
Loop 3-4 chords without strong tonic return. Harmonic interest = rhythmic placement + blue notes.
Rhythmic displacement (Dilla feel) affects perceived harmonic resolution — same chord sounds unresolved when hit late.
Vocal clusters are the extended chord voicings — the top note of the harmony lives in the melody, not the keyboard.

leon_thomas.name: Leon Thomas
leon_thomas.description: F-minor-family modal vamps built on stacked m7/m9/m11 extensions with deliberate V7 avoidance, bVII as the harmonic breath point, and sparse inner voicing that leaves register space open for falsetto and stacked self-harmonies.

progressions.label: MUTT — F minor modal vamp
progressions.notes: Fm7 – Bbm7 – Eb – C (or Cm). Eb (bVII) acts as the harmonic breath before returning — Mixolydian/Dorian marker. C used without raised leading tone, preserving modal ambiguity. No V7 resolution.

progressions.label: YES IT IS — jazz ii-V-i with bVI landing
progressions.notes: Gm7 – C7 – Fm7 – Dbmaj7 in F minor. Textbook ii–V–i but lands on Dbmaj7 (bVI maj7) instead of resolving back — creates floating, unresolved quality. The bVI landing is the signature alt-R&B move.

progressions.label: VIBES DON'T LIE — dominant tension stack
progressions.notes: Bbm7 – Ebm7 – Gb7 – F7 in Bb minor. Gb7 (bVI7) is a borrowed dominant adding tritone tension without jazz secondary dominant voice leading. COLORS Show version uses Fm7 for F7, returning to a non-dominant landing.

progressions.label: LOVE JONES — descending minor thirds with dominant 7 coloring
progressions.notes: Cm7 – Ab7 – Db7 – Fm7 in Ab/F minor. Db7 is a tritone sub of G7 (V of Cm). Cycle of descending minor thirds with dominant 7th coloring on non-diatonic roots — straight jazz reharmonization in R&B production.

progressions.label: FAR FETCHED — minor to major parallel shift
progressions.notes: G#m – F#m – F# – E7. F#m to F# major is a parallel major/minor swap — major 3rd swapped within same root. Neo-soul color technique. bVI7 (E7) as color chord closing the phrase.

rules: Default key center: flat-key minor (Bb minor, F minor, Ab minor). Avoid bright major keys except for rare color contrast.
Avoid V7 resolution. bVII major is the primary rest point — not a dominant.
Keep modal ambiguity between Dorian, Aeolian, and Mixolydian — never fully commit to one mode.
Omit the 5th in voicings — keep b3, b7, 9 in a mid-register inner cluster. Leave top voice open above the 12th for vocals.
bVI maj7 landing (Dbmaj7 in F minor) is the signature unresolved closure — use it instead of resolving to tonic.
ii–V–i jazz turnarounds are acceptable if they land on a bVI maj7 rather than fully resolving.
Sparse comping is structural — leave room for falsetto ad libs in the upper register.

glasper.name: Robert Glasper
glasper.description: Rootless cluster voicings with minor 2nds as deliberate crunch, cyclic third-motion instead of ii-V-I, and a left hand that locks to 16th-note syncopation like an MPC pattern — jazz brain on a hip-hop nervous system.

progressions.label: No Worries — cyclic thirds ascending
progressions.notes: Fm11 – Abmaj9 – Cm11 – Dbmaj13(#11). Root motion ascending by thirds: Db–F–Ab–C–Eb–G–Bb–D. Dbmaj13(#11) is Lydian-flavored (#11 = G natural over Db) — floating, non-resolving quality.

progressions.label: Rise and Shine — cSLIDE and extended motion
progressions.notes: Fmaj9 – Dmaj9 – Dbmaj9 – Gm11 – C7 – Gbmaj9 – Bbm9 – Dm11 – C7alt – F7sus4. Gbmaj9 is maximally remote from F tonic (cSLIDE half-step move). C7alt = C dominant with b9/#9/b13.

progressions.label: Afro Blue (Black Radio) — pure major-third cycle
progressions.notes: Cm – Abmaj7 – Ebmaj7 – Dbmaj7. Root motion by major 3rds: C→Ab→Eb→Db. Pure cyclic third motion — no dominant, no resolution. This is the most teachable example of Glasper's harmonic architecture.

progressions.label: Ah Yeah — descending maj7 chain
progressions.notes: Bbm7 – Abmaj7 – Gbmaj7 – Fmaj7. Descending maj7 chain through non-functional keys. Characteristic neo-soul shared vocabulary (also Badu, Bilal). Chord complexity 97/100.

progressions.label: Neo-soul loop — Dorian vamp with gospel slash
progressions.notes: Bb-11 → G-9 → Gbmaj13 → Eb-11. Cyclic loop with stepwise bass motion. Also: G-9 | D-9 | A-11 | Bbmaj7 Fadd2/A — the Fadd2/A slash voicing is a direct gospel inheritance.

progressions.label: Descending chromatic minor 9 vamp
progressions.notes: Gm9 → F#m9 → Fm9 — minor 9 voicings descending by half steps. Produces the characteristic 'sliding darkness' of Black Radio-era tracks. Left hand ostinato pattern.

rules: Move by thirds (ic3/4 cycle), not by fourths/fifths. Replace ii-V-I with third-related root motion.
Rootless cluster voicings: omit root, stack 3rd + 7th + 9th + 11th in close position (within a 5th span). Minor 2nds are deliberate crunch, not errors.
Replace V7 with 13sus4 or sus2 — no leading tone, no resolution urge. This is the single biggest difference from bebop.
Left hand locks to 16th-note syncopation like an MPC pattern — NOT jazz-style beat 2 and 4 comping.
cSLIDE: parallel half-step shift of entire chord for modal mixture — preserves 7 common tones.
Default minor mode is Dorian (natural 6th). Chromatic passing tones approach chord tones on beat 1.
Rootless voicings need a bass layer to define the root — without it, voicings are harmonically ambiguous.
Lydian dominant (#11 on V) and upper structure triads (bVI triad over V7 shell) for dominants when you need them.

badu.name: Erykah Badu
badu.description: Cyclic minor extensions and non-diatonic major 7th substitutions filtered through the Soulquarians' jazz-meets-gospel voicing ethic — jazz harmony in color, stripped of its resolution compulsion, with Bb Dorian as the primary modal center.

progressions.label: Other Side of the Game — chromatic mediant chorus
progressions.notes: Dbmaj9 → Abmaj9 → Emaj7 → Eb+#9/A in Ab major. bVI (Emaj7) is a non-diatonic chromatic mediant substitution — a Badu signature. V chord appears as an augmented slash, never resolves classically. Slash chords as modal-jazz nod.

progressions.label: On and On — Aeolian i-iv-v loop
progressions.notes: Em7 – Am7 – Bm (i–iv–v in E minor). Minor v, not major V — no leading tone, no dominant resolution. Pure Aeolian natural minor. Melody floats over without resolving tension.

progressions.label: Tyrone — Bb minor chromatic mediant loop
progressions.notes: Bbm9 → D#m9 → E/F#. Also charted as Amaj7 → Bbm7 → Ebm7 → Gbm7. Modal static looping — groove stays on Bbm extended for long stretches. 100/100 chord complexity on Hooktheory.

progressions.label: Green Eyes — fully jazz-voiced suite
progressions.notes: G9b13 – Am/C – B7 – Bbmaj7 – A7 – Dm/A – Ebdim/A – Emaj7. G9b13 is dominant with b13 alteration. Ebdim/A is a diminished passing chord. Emaj7 is a non-diatonic chromatic mediant (bII in D minor). Roy Hargrove's trumpet stacked 2-3x for cluster texture.

progressions.label: Window Seat — Bb Dorian with full extension palette
progressions.notes: Bb Dorian — natural minor with raised 6th (G natural). Bb Dorian permits EbMaj7 on the IV chord without leaving the minor world. Confirmed use of seventh, secondary, altered, inverted, extended, sus2/sus4, add, borrowed chords. 99/100 chord complexity.

progressions.label: Cyclic loop — Soulquarians shared vocabulary
progressions.notes: Bb-11 → G-9 → Gbmaj13 → Eb-11. Harmonic gravity is centripetal, not directional — loops, doesn't cadence. Stepwise bass motion. Direct shared vocabulary with Glasper.

rules: Avoid V→I resolution. Loop minor extensions (m9, m11) and use slash chords and maj7 non-diatonic substitutions instead.
Bb Dorian is the default modal center on later records — minor with a natural 6th, which permits IVmaj7 without leaving minor territory.
Non-diatonic bVI maj7 chord (e.g., Emaj7 in Ab, Dbmaj7 in Bb minor) is a signature chromatic mediant substitution — not a passing chord.
Slash chords (Abm7/Gb, Bbm9/Eb) function modally, not as inversions — they create ambiguity between two key centers.
Phrygian bII borrowed chord appears as a one-chord color moment — not a structural move.
Gospel 'lift': major IV in minor context (Gmaj7 in D minor) for an emotional breath without leaving the minor world.
Vocal harmonies imply the extensions the keyboard doesn't state — soprano doubles 9th or 11th of the chord.
Harmonic stasis (4-8 bars on one chord with rhythmic/vocal variation) is the structural device, not chord movement.

paak.name: Anderson .Paak / Silk Sonic
paak.description: 70s Philadelphia/Motown soul harmony — non-functional extended 7ths, dominant sus voicings, chromatic mediant modulations, and quartal structures — filtered through a drummer's instinct for off-beat harmonic placement and short, vamping progressions.

progressions.label: Leave the Door Open — verse (F Lydian feel)
progressions.notes: Fmaj7 – G7/F – Em7 – Am7 in A minor. G7/F = dominant with F in bass — floating, rootless quality, the progression never lands on C tonic. Feels like F Lydian starting on IV.

progressions.label: Leave the Door Open — pre-chorus (Eb modulation)
progressions.notes: Ebmaj7 – Gm7 – Fm7 – Abmaj7 in Eb major. Sudden modulation to Eb at ~0:40. Functions as I–III–II–IV.

progressions.label: Leave the Door Open — bridge (chromatic mediant descent)
progressions.notes: Abmaj9 – G7sus4 – Gbmaj9 – F7sus4. Chromatic mediant descent — whole tone drop. G11 chords at 2:27-2:33. Outro modulates up a semitone to B minor: Amaj7 – G#11 – C#maj7 – Bb7b9.

progressions.label: Blast Off — quartal sus vocabulary
progressions.notes: B13sus – G#13sus (minor-3rd transposition). Primary resolution: B13sus → Emaj7. Coda: Amaj7 – Emaj7 – G#min7 – D#7sus – F7sus. D#7sus substitutes expected D#ø7 — likely unique in pop. Quartal harmony: stacks 4ths, tritone becomes perfect 4th, everything floats.

progressions.label: Skate — G minor Dorian vamp with chromatic slides
progressions.notes: Gm7 – C7 – Am7 – D7 (ii-V in C, starting on vi — ambiguity between G Dorian and C major). Also: B6 – C6 (chromatic half-step major 6th shift) and Emaj – Fmaj (half-step parallel major pair — P-Funk signature).

progressions.label: Come Down — Bb minor bVI vamp
progressions.notes: Bbm – Bbm – Gbmaj – Fm. Two bars of tonic minor, then bVI (Gbmaj) as chromatic-third pivot, iv (Fm) for minor plagal close. Chord change on 'and' of 2 — not a downbeat. Drummer's harmonic rhythm.

rules: Dominant 7ths and extended chords (13th, 13sus4) are resting tones, not tension — they don't need to resolve.
The 7th is the primary color tone of funk harmony. Maj7 on major, min7 on minor — standard issue.
Quartal sus chords (B13sus) stack 4ths — different from sus4 which just replaces the 3rd. Both float; quartal has more open color.
Chromatic mediant modulations (key shifts by a 3rd) are the primary structural key changes — not pivot-chord modulation.
Half-step parallel chord slides (Emaj → Fmaj, B6 → C6) require no diatonic justification — smooth voice leading does the work.
Chord changes fall on upbeats ('and' of 2 or 4), not downbeats — drummer's instinct applied to harmonic rhythm.
Short 2-4 chord vamps repeated 8-16 times. Groove creates tension; the chord doesn't have to.
Tritone substitution (Bb7b9 for E7 in Leave the Door Open outro) is a jazz-inherited tool for maximum harmonic tension with melody preserved.

stevie_wonder.name: Stevie Wonder
stevie_wonder.description: Dominant 9(sus4) as the signature soft dominant, Dorian two-chord funk vamps, jazz-extended harmony applied to gospel voice-leading logic, and abrupt chromatic/half-step modulations in place of classical pivot-chord transitions.

progressions.label: Isn't She Lovely — vi-II-V9sus-I
progressions.notes: C#m – F#9 – B9sus4 – E in E major. F#9 is V/V (secondary dominant). B9sus4 delays resolution — the gospel 'held breath' effect before landing on I. Most teachable example of his signature dominant substitution.

progressions.label: I Wish — Eb Dorian two-chord vamp
progressions.notes: Ebm – Abmaj7. The IVmaj7 over a Dorian tonic is the defining Dorian sound: natural 6th (C natural in Eb Dorian) appears in Abmaj7, distinguishing it from Aeolian. Minimal — groove IS the composition.

progressions.label: Golden Lady — verse ascending scale with #iv substitution
progressions.notes: EbMaj9 – Fm9 – Gm7 – A7 – D7 in Eb major. A7 (#iv7) replaces the expected IV chord — 'rising wave' that subverts the ascending scale. V7/III (D7) resolves into pre-chorus. Chorus uses bIIMaj7 (AbMaj7) for 'mysterious lift.'

progressions.label: As — intro and verse with gospel I7→IV move
progressions.notes: Bmaj7 – B7 – Emaj7 (intro). B7 treats tonic as its own dominant to push to IV — a gospel move. Verse: Bmaj7 – Amaj7 (bVIIMaj7, Mixolydian borrow) – G#m – A#m – D#7 – G#m7 – G#m6 – C#m7 – F#7.

progressions.label: As — bridge chromatic mediant
progressions.notes: Emaj7 – Bmaj7 – Dmaj9. Dmaj9 (bIIIMaj9) is a chromatic mediant — major third below B, borrowed from parallel Dorian or direct chromatic mediant. Gospel 'lift' before returning.

progressions.label: You Are the Sunshine of My Life — bossa nova ii-V9sus loop
progressions.notes: B – E – D#m – G#m – C#m – F#9sus4 – B in B major. G#m as VI (secondary dominant area) before ii–V9sus–I resolution. G#m functions as V of ii (C#m). F#9sus4 is the signature soft dominant close.

progressions.label: Superstition — Eb Dorian/minor two-bar vamp
progressions.notes: Ebm7 | Ab7/Eb. Ab7/Eb = IV7 in Bb Dorian, not a functioning dominant. The entire track is variations on this two-bar Eb minor pentatonic vamp. 8-16 Clavinet tracks layered.

rules: The 9sus4 chord (root–P4–P5–b7–M9) is the signature dominant substitute — softens cadential pull without tritone tension.
Dorian two-chord vamps (i – IVmaj7) are the funk groove foundation. The major IV over minor tonic is the Dorian sound marker.
Chromatic/half-step modulations are abrupt and unprepared — no pivot chord. Two half-step modulations in one song (Golden Lady) is standard.
I7 → IV (treating tonic as its own dominant) is a gospel move that creates forward momentum without leaving the key.
bIIMaj7 (Neapolitan maj7) functions as a color chord resolving by half-step down, not as a dominant.
bVIIMaj7 (Mixolydian borrow) adds modal color in otherwise major-key songs.
Secondary dominant chains (V7/III, V7/ii, V7/vi) are used to delay resolution, not complete it — suspend the listener.
Black key centers (Eb, Db, Ab, Gb) are the native register — voicings cluster here on the Clavinet and Rhodes.

herbie_hancock.name: Herbie Hancock
herbie_hancock.description: Quartal/quintal poly-chord voicings with the Rhodes locked to the midrange while the ARP or clavinet covers bass/rhythm, Dorian modal vamps where dominants are color chords not resolutions, and a narrow per-track chord palette where harmonic interest comes from rhythmic placement and voice-leading.

progressions.label: Maiden Voyage — quartal sus vamp prototype
progressions.notes: Dsus7 (Am7/D) – Fsus7 (Cm7/F) – Ebsus7 (Bbm7/Eb) – C#m7(11). Each chord: minor 7th chord with bass sitting a perfect 4th above the chord root — readable as stacked 4ths all the way up. Template for all modal comping through the fusion era.

progressions.label: Chameleon — Bb Dorian i-IV modal vamp
progressions.notes: Bbm7 – Eb7. Eb7 is IV7 in Bb Dorian — NOT a functioning dominant, it is a Mixolydian color chord. The vamp never resolves. ARP Odyssey handles bass; Rhodes plays upper-register clusters in the midrange.

progressions.label: Actual Proof — same Bb Dorian structure with Lydian passing color
progressions.notes: Bbm7 – Eb7 main vamp. Bar 12: Ebmaj7#11/D — #11 (A) in melody line over D bass. Lydian Dominant color appearing as passing harmony during voice-leading. Bar 5 may be Bbmin9 or Gbmaj7.

progressions.label: Am11 quartal poly-chord shape
progressions.notes: A–E (LH spread 5th) / C–D–G–B (RH: b3, 11, b7, 9). Six notes total. Alternatively: LH = Am triad spread, RH = Gmaj second inversion. Diatonic transposition up a 3rd → Cmaj7(#11). Further → D7(#11) — Lydian Dominant as byproduct of quartal transposition.

progressions.label: Descending minor 9th half-step vamp
progressions.notes: Dm9 → Dbm9 → Cm9. Minor 9th voicings descending by half steps while right hand plays Dorian mode lines with chromatic passing tones. Bebop-to-neo-soul bridge technique.

rules: Quartal voicing: LH = root + 5th (spread), RH = b3 + 11 + b7 + 9 as a tight cluster. Defines the midrange Rhodes sound.
ARP Odyssey or clavinet handles bass/low register — Rhodes stays in E3–D5 range doing color work, not root-defining work.
Dominant chords (Eb7 in Bb Dorian) are modal color chords — they do NOT resolve. This is the essential distinction from bebop.
Sus chord removes tritone from dominant — chords sit as static groove-locked color without creating expectation of resolution.
Narrow chord palette per track (often just Dorian i-IV). Harmonic interest comes from rhythmic placement and voice-leading, not chord substitution.
Lydian Dominant (#11) appears as a byproduct of quartal transposition — do not force it as a deliberate modal choice.
Clavinet plays strummed quartal 4th-interval patterns as rhythmic comping (guitar substitute), not harmonic color.
ARP Odyssey is monophonic — it plays single-note bass and lead lines only, never chords.

thundercat.name: Thundercat
thundercat.description: Diatonic extended chords (min9, maj9, min11) built on shell voicings from a 6-string bass, punctuated by one non-diatonic 'wild card' chord (tritone sub or minor-3rd drop), connected by chromatic approach bass lines that function as voice-leading.

progressions.label: Friend Zone — common-tone tritone insertion
progressions.notes: Dm9 | Dbmaj7. D minor to Db major — the wild card tritone-adjacent drop. Both chords share F and C as common tones. The shared tones are the voice-leading glue that makes the distant root motion work.

progressions.label: Them Changes — chromatic approach then tonic
progressions.notes: Cbmaj7 | Gm7 | Abm7 | Fm7 | Ebm11 in Eb minor (VI – IIIm – IVm – IIm – Im). Gm7 is a chromatic approach to Abm7, not diatonic. Tonic arrival on Ebm11 at the end.

progressions.label: Dragonball Durag — ascending diatonic bass line
progressions.notes: Em7 – F#m7 – Gmaj7 – Asus4 – Bm7sus4 in D major. Fully diatonic ascending bass line (D, E, F#, G, A). Turnaround: C#m7b5 – F#7 (IIm7b5 – V7, resolves to Dmaj7).

progressions.label: Unrequited Love — diatonic then tritone wild card
progressions.notes: Gm9 | Cm9 | Gm9 | Gbmaj7. Diatonic Gm9 to Cm9 and back, then the knockout card: Gbmaj7, a tritone down from the tonic. The characteristic 'wild card' non-diatonic insertion.

progressions.label: Show You the Way — diatonic descending IV-III-VII-VI in F
progressions.notes: Bbmaj7 – Am7 – Em7 – Dm7 in F major. Straightforward diatonic descending motion. Voicings and bass line are what make it feel sophisticated, not harmonic complexity.

progressions.label: How Sway — chromatic minor 9 ascent
progressions.notes: Cm9 → Dbm9 → Dm9 → Em9. Double-tracked bass climbing chromatically. Each chord is a minor 9 voicing. Chromatic root motion across a minor-third span.

rules: Shell voicing on bass: root + 3rd + 7th, omit the 5th. Add one extension (9th, 11th, or 13th) to push to extended color.
The wild card: write a fully diatonic progression, then insert one non-diatonic chord (tritone sub or minor-3rd drop from tonic) as the signature move.
Chromatic approach bass: connect root-to-root via half-step passing tones, from above (half-step down) and below (half-step up).
Extension octave placement is non-negotiable — 13th voiced close to root sounds harsh; an octave up it sounds lush.
Parallel major/minor pivot: shift from minor to parallel major within a single chord for one-chord reharmonization.
Bass functions as root definition, chromatic approach, AND rhythmic syncopation (16th-note anticipation) simultaneously.
Extensions as melody carriers: chord extensions (13th on Gm13 = melody note E) sit on top of the voicing, one octave above the 5th.
Do not worry about chords fitting within a key — ensure the melody notes are contained within the chords.

gospel.name: Gospel
gospel.description: Same extended chord vocabulary as jazz but with plagal (IV–I) cadences as the primary motion, suspended dominants as resting states not tension, and V7 frequently avoided or suspended — sacred harmony that resolves by descent, not by tritone pull.

progressions.label: I Smile (Kirk Franklin) — verse with secondary dominant
progressions.notes: C – D7/C – F – Bbsus2 in C major. D7/C = secondary dominant (V/V) with C pedal point in bass. Bbsus2 as resting IV color chord — sus2 ambiguates major/minor on the IV.

progressions.label: I Smile (Kirk Franklin) — chorus with bVII and suspended tonic
progressions.notes: C – Csus4 – F – Bb – Gsus4. Csus4 immediately re-suspends tonic. Bb = bVII (borrowed from C Mixolydian). Gsus4 hangs without resolution — intentionally unresolved ending.

progressions.label: No Weapon (Fred Hammond) — gospel chorus with descending bass
progressions.notes: Abadd9 – Eb/G – Fm7 – Eb – DbMaj7 – Ab/C – Bbm7 in Ab major. Descending chromatic bass: Ab–G–F. DbMaj7 = bIVmaj7, borrowed subdominant with maj7 for 'sacred' floating quality. Bbm7 = ii chord sets up deceptive return rather than V-I authentic cadence.

progressions.label: Let the Praise Begin (Fred Hammond) — double plagal loop
progressions.notes: Bb – Eb – Ab – Eb – Bb. The Ab (bVII) is the backdoor dominant functioning as color before plagal IV return. Named 'double plagal' — IV approached plagally from its own IV. Signature gospel ending/turnaround.

progressions.label: mMaj7 line-cliché — chromatic inner voice descent
progressions.notes: C-Eb-G → C-Eb-G-B → C-Eb-G-Bb → C-Eb-G-A. Bass holds on C. Only the 7th changes: maj7 → min7 → maj6. Chromatic descending line in the 7th voice. Common in gospel intros and modulation passages.

progressions.label: Gospel sus polychord — standard worship band sound
progressions.notes: LH: root + 5th of V (G-D in key of C). RH: IV triad (F-A-C). Net result = G11 or Gsus4/9. Thick, suspended, unresolved — the standard 'worship band sus sound.' Used in Fred Hammond ballads and Kirk Franklin meditative passages.

rules: Primary cadence is plagal (IV→I), not authentic (V7→I). The 'Amen' cadence is structural.
Sus chords (V7sus4, Vsus2) are resting states — they can be the final chord of a phrase, not just tension before V7.
bVII chord (backdoor dominant) resolves to I from a whole step below — softer pull than authentic V-I, used as a color/texture chord.
Double plagal (bVII→IV→I) is the signature gospel ending/turnaround.
IVadd9 preferred over IVmaj7 on the tonic level — retains openness without the jazz-flavored maj7 interval.
V7(b9) is the 'gospel sting' — places b9 in the right hand over 3+b7 shell in left. Strong pull to tonic.
mMaj7 is used only in line-cliché passing motion (im → immaj7 → im7 → im6) — never as a static resting chord.
Choir stacking creates implied maj9 voicings: bass=root, tenor=7th, alto=9th, soprano=5th or 3rd.

dilla.name: J Dilla
dilla.description: Harmony created at the sample selection stage — harmonically ambiguous loop points from jazz-soul sources, composed as fugues of independent melodic lines rather than vertical stacks, with rhythmic displacement blurring chord transitions across bar lines.

progressions.label: Workinonit — E minor Aeolian rock cadence
progressions.notes: Cmaj – Dmaj – Em (VI–VII–i in E minor). Chopped from 10cc's 'The Worst Band in the World.' Source's tonal context disrupted — never revealed for longer than a few seconds. Aeolian modal countermelody in the bass implies natural minor against the surface rock cadence.

progressions.label: Two Can Win — Bb major relative ambiguity
progressions.notes: Gm – Bbmaj7 – Bbmaj – Dm7 in Bb major / G minor. Relative major/minor ambiguity never resolved — Dilla leaves both readings open simultaneously. From The Sylvers 'Only One Can Win' (gospel-adjacent soul source).

progressions.label: Didn't Cha Know (Badu) — Bb minor jazz-funk
progressions.notes: Ebm7 – Bb7 – Dbmaj – Fm7 in Bb minor. Source: Tarika Blue 'Dreamflower' (Ab minor, pitched up a whole step). The Bb7 is the one functioning dominant — unlike most Dilla tracks this one has some resolution pull.

rules: Harmony lives in loop point selection — which 4 bars of the source record you loop IS the harmonic decision.
Single structural source per track. Additional samples are decorative surface, not harmonic conflict.
Prefer harmonically ambiguous loop points — transitional bars, incomplete chord voicings, suspended moments (open 4ths, unresolved 7ths).
Compose chords as fugues of independent melodic lines (bass, inner voice, upper voice from separate chops) — the chord is heard retrospectively, not played simultaneously.
Half-speed pitch shifting is a reharmonization tool — slowing a sample changes its key center, mode, and rhythmic feel in one edit.
Rhythmic displacement creates harmonic ambiguity — a chord fragment landing on the 'and' of beat 4 reads as anticipation or sustained depending on pocket.
Preferred harmonic modes: Dorian minor (Gm7/Cm7 with raised 6th implied), Aeolian natural minor, major with added 6th/9th.
The 'drunk timing' myth is false — drum placement is deliberate MPC swing/shift programming creating polyrhythmic grid.

kendrick.name: Kendrick Lamar
kendrick.description: Minor/Dorian modal centers with heavy bVI maj7 as a color landing point, suspended dominants without resolution, extended voicings throughout driven by a live jazz ensemble improvising over drum and bass foundations.

progressions.label: These Walls — F major/minor parallel toggle
progressions.notes: Abmaj9 – Gm7 – Gm7/C – Fmaj7 in F major. F minor section: Dbmaj7 – Cm7 – Fm7 – C7. Dbmaj7 = bVI maj7 in F minor — characteristic TPAB sound. Gm7/C = ii7/V without resolving to I — suspended dominant function. 96/100 chord complexity and novelty.

progressions.label: How Much a Dollar Cost — Bb minor bVI modal vamp
progressions.notes: Gbmaj7 – Fmin – Gbmaj7 – Abmaj in Bb minor. Gbmaj7 (bVI maj7) is not passing — it is the harmonic landing point. Polyrhythmic combination of 3/4 and 4/4. Mournful shuffle feel.

progressions.label: King Kunta — E minor Dorian-adjacent funk
progressions.notes: Em – Am – Bm (i–iv–v, all minor, no major V) in E minor. Dorian-adjacent: absence of major V and flat-7 over the groove. Half-step key shift to F minor in verses 1 and 2 — downward chromatic modulation instead of dominant resolution.

progressions.label: Wesley's Theory — B minor P-Funk modal vamp
progressions.notes: G – Bm – F#m – Em – F# in B minor. One-chord-vamp modal funk skeleton (Bm modal center). P-Funk philosophy: unresolved minor 7th groove that parks and refuses to go home. Thundercat's bass implies 9th/11th territory.

progressions.label: You Ain't Gotta Lie — jazz ii-V-i in B minor
progressions.notes: Cmaj7 – Bmin7 – Emin7 – F#7 in B minor. Cmaj7-Bmin7 = bIIMaj7 → im7 half-step chromatic descent (Neapolitan function). F#7 as V7 = one of the few TPAB tracks with functional dominant resolution.

progressions.label: Complexion — i-VII-VI-VII Motown-derived
progressions.notes: Jackson 5-derived harmonic movement (I Want You Back pattern) in F# minor. The track 'restrains itself, melting into mellow jazz harmony.' Thundercat's extended chord vocabulary inflects the rhythm section.

rules: bVI maj7 (Gbmaj7 in Bb minor, Dbmaj7 in F minor) is the most distinctively TPAB harmonic color — a borrowed major chord functioning as a landing, not a passing chord.
Suspended dominants (ii7/V without resolution) are structural — Gm7/C implies dominant function but suspends it indefinitely.
Half-step modulation (E→F minor, E→Eb) is the structural key change tool — replaces conventional tonal development.
P-Funk principle on groove tracks: park on minor 7th modal vamp, refuse to resolve. Groove IS the harmony.
Live ensemble improvisation over drum/bass foundations — voicings are emergent from jazz-literate musicians, not pre-specified.
Parallel major/minor toggle at the section level (F major ↔ F minor) is borrowed chord technique applied structurally.
Functional dominant V7→i reserved for specific emotional moments (You Ain't Gotta Lie) — not the default.
bIIMaj7 (Neapolitan maj7) as half-step chromatic descent into minor tonic — jazz reharmonization applied to hip-hop.

mac_miller.name: Mac Miller
mac_miller.description: Jazz-rooted neo-soul harmony — secondary dominant chains, bVII major borrowing, and bIII maj7 in minor contexts — filtered through Jon Brion's film-scoring aesthetic of cinematic unresolved tension, with simple melodies floating over harmonically complex ground.

progressions.label: Come Back to Earth — full secondary dominant chain in C major
progressions.notes: Cmaj7 – C7 – Fmaj7 – Em7 – A7 – Bbmaj7 – Dm7 – E7 – Am7 – Bb – Dm. C7 = I7 treating tonic as dominant of IV. A7 = V7/ii. Bbmaj7 = bVII maj7 (Mixolydian borrow). E7 = V7/vi. Full chain: C7→Fmaj7→Em7→A7→Bbmaj7→Dm7→E7→Am7.

progressions.label: Small Worlds — Bb minor neo-soul ii-V with altered dominant
progressions.notes: Fmaj7 – Gm7 – C7b9 in Bb minor (bVI – bVII – V7b9). Jazz ii-V adapted for minor key with b9 extension as the jazz fingerprint. Also: Fmaj7 – Gm7 – A7 – C7 in some sections. 96/100 chord complexity, 98/100 novelty.

progressions.label: Ladders — G minor neo-soul minor bVI
progressions.notes: Cm7 – Ebmaj7 – Gm7 in G minor. Cm7 = im7, Ebmaj7 = bIIImaj7 (relative major with 7th — jazz minor borrowed chord), Gm7 = vm7. Textbook neo-soul minor vocabulary.

progressions.label: Circles (title) — E major with bVII color
progressions.notes: E – Esus4 – D6 – A – D – D7 in A major (or E major). D6 = bVII with added 6th (softer than Dmaj7 — D6 is D–F#–A–B, not D–F#–A–C#). D7 left without resolution — Jon Brion's unresolved tension aesthetic.

progressions.label: Jet Fuel — C minor with Phrygian-inflected II
progressions.notes: Gm – Cm – D – Cm in C minor. D major (bII or harmonic minor II) is non-diatonic — Phrygian inflection or harmonic minor V substitution. Likely C Dorian with D major as chromatic II chord.

progressions.label: A Moment 4 Jazz (Larry Lovestein) — ii-V-I in C
progressions.notes: Am7 – G7 – C7 – F in C major. Foundational jazz vocabulary predating Swimming by 6 years — confirms Larry Lovestein-era jazz fluency.

rules: bVII major (Bb in C major, D in A major) is the most consistent harmonic color — Mixolydian borrowing that gives tracks a floating quality rather than V→I finality.
bIII maj7 (Ebmaj7 in G or C minor) is the jazz minor standard — relative major chord with added 7th.
Secondary dominant chains (I7→IV, VI7→ii, III7→vi) are structural, not ornamental — follow the chain through multiple moves.
D7b9 (V7b9) is the genre's most powerful tension chord into minor tonic — the b9 is the jazz fingerprint.
Unresolved dominant tension is an aesthetic choice — D7 in Circles is intentionally left hanging.
Simple melody over complex harmony — pentatonic/diatonic vocal line stays inside while the chords go outside.
Rootless voicings are common — bass covers the root, upper chord voices handle 3rd/7th/9th stack in mid-register.
D6 and Dmaj7 are different colors — D6 (D–F#–A–B) is softer and more neo-soul; Dmaj7 (D–F#–A–C#) is jazzier. Use D6 as default.

joe_pass.name: Joe Pass
joe_pass.description: Shell voicing, drop-3, and drop-2 mixed fluidly within a single chord change — guide tones (3rd and 7th) are the fixed harmonic identity, chromatic approach chords and tritone substitutions target the V7 in ii-V-I, and inner voice movement creates motion inside otherwise static chords.

progressions.label: ii-V-I in C — drop-3 with bass note
progressions.notes: Dm7 (D/A-C-F) – G7 (G/D-F-B) – Cmaj7 (C/G-B-E). Left hand = single bass note, right hand = 3-note upper cluster from drop-3. Tritone (B-F) present in G7 shell — the irreducible harmonic identity of V7.

progressions.label: ii-V-I in C — rootless drop-2 (with bass player)
progressions.notes: Dm7 (F-A-C-E = 3-5-7-9) – G7 (F-B-D-A = b7-3-5-9 or 13) – Cmaj7 (E-G-B-D = 3-5-7-9). No root in any voicing. Extensions added naturally: 9th on Dm7, 9th or 13th on G7, 9th on Cmaj7.

progressions.label: ii-V-I in C with tritone substitution
progressions.notes: Dm7 – Db7 – Cmaj7. G7 replaced by Db7 (tritone sub). Db7 bass moves down by half-step to C — strong smooth voice leading. Guide tones of G7 (B and F) = guide tones of Db7 (F and Cb/B), roles swapped.

progressions.label: Chromatic approach descent with sus resolution
progressions.notes: Ab13sus4 → Ab13 → G13sus4 → G13 → Gb13sus4 → Gb13. Half-step-per-bar descent with sus4→resolved dominant motion each step. Sus voicing = 9-5-1 in right hand over 1-b7 in left; resolves to standard dominant 3-7.

progressions.label: I to ii chromatic passing diminished
progressions.notes: Cmaj7 → C#dim7 → Dm7. Chromatic bass motion — dim7 fills the gap between I and ii. C#dim7 = Ebdim7 = Gbdim7 = Adim7 (symmetric, one grip, four functions). Also usable between IV and V: Fmaj7 → F#dim7 → G7.

rules: Three base voicing types: shell (R-3-7), drop-3 (bass note + 3-note upper cluster), drop-2 (2-note LH + 2-note RH). Mix fluidly — don't stay in one system.
Guide tones (3rd and 7th) NEVER move between voicing systems — only surrounding notes change position.
V7 shell always contains the tritone (3rd + b7). These two notes are the irreducible harmonic identity of any dominant chord.
Tritone substitution targets V7 in ii-V-I — replace G7 with Db7. Bass descends by half-step for smooth voice leading.
Passing diminished 7ths are symmetric — one grip covers all four enharmonic functions. Use between any diatonic chord and its neighbor a whole step away.
Chromatic approach chords: approach any target chord from a half-step above or below using the same chord type — parallel motion.
Do not harmonize every note. Harmonize structurally important notes (downbeats, phrase starts/ends, long tones) and leave runs as single-note lines.
Inner voice movement creates motion inside a static chord — one voice moves chromatically while bass and melody hold.

ama_lou.name: Ama Lou
ama_lou.description: Functional minor tonality with selective jazz extensions — closer to Sade's harmonic restraint than US neo-soul modal density — built on the bVI major plateau, harmonic minor V7 for tension, and an intuitive Dorian/Aeolian toggle without committing to either mode.

progressions.label: Tried Up — harmonic minor V with bVI landing
progressions.notes: Am7 – E7 – Fmaj7 – G7 in A minor. E7 (V7 with raised 3rd) is borrowed from harmonic minor — the signature neo-soul tension move. Fmaj7 (bVI) provides borrowed major color plateau. Also documented: E-7, F-maj7, F-maj6, A-min7, Em, B-hdim7 voicings.

progressions.label: Northside — i-V-VI-V in B minor
progressions.notes: Bm – F# – G – F# in B minor. Clean folk-influenced minor. Gmaj7 extension adds neo-soul warmth without departing diatonic territory. F#-7 (F# minor 7) softens the dominant pull — a key UK-soul move avoiding hard resolution.

progressions.label: Trust Nobody — i-bVII-IV-bIII minor pentatonic
progressions.notes: C#m – B – F# – E in C# minor. bVII (B major) and bIII (E major) borrowed from Dorian/Aeolian overlap. Most purely minor-pentatonic-sounding progression in her catalog.

progressions.label: This Town — bVI-v-i-V7 in F minor
progressions.notes: Db – Cm – Fm – C7 in F minor. Cm (minor v) avoids leading tone — keeps it modal-Aeolian. C7 as harmonic minor tension before returning to Db bVI. Db (bVI) is the borrowed major color plateau present throughout her catalog.

progressions.label: Wire — i-bVII-bVI-V in B minor
progressions.notes: Bm – A – G – F# in B minor. Identical structural type to Northside — i, bVII, bVI cadences are her default minor formula. Functional resolution to F# (V) closes it.

progressions.label: TBC — Dorian/Aeolian toggle in C minor
progressions.notes: Cm – Ab – F – Fm – Eb. The appearance of both Fm (minor iv, Aeolian) and F major (major IV, Dorian) within the same song is the key textural device — toggling between Aeolian and Dorian gives the track its ambiguity.

progressions.label: Patience — i-bVII-bVI-ii in F# minor
progressions.notes: F#m – E – D – G#m in F# minor. G#m (ii) is a diatonic minor second degree — relatively rare in her catalog, adds a jazz-adjacent move. Slow 80 BPM suits extended voice-leading space.

rules: Default tonality is functional minor. Extensions are selective color additions, not structural foundations.
bVI major plateau (Ab in Cm, Db in Fm, G in Bm) is the signature harmonic resting point — appears in nearly every song.
Harmonic minor V7 (E7 in Am, C7 in Fm, F# in Bm) creates controlled tension — follow it with bVI rather than resolving to tonic for a deceptive-ish move.
Dorian/Aeolian toggle: shift between minor iv (Fm) and major IV (F) freely within the same song — felt, not theorized.
Static or slow-moving harmony. 2-4 chord loops repeated throughout the song — prioritize melodic and vocal development over harmonic motion.
7th extensions are add-on color (Gmaj7 over a Bm progression) — not structural jazz chord function.
Half-diminished (B-hdim7 in Tried Up) used as a passing chord or substitution in fills/transitions — not a static chord.
Vocal harmonies double chord tones (3rds, 5ths) rather than jazz extensions — keeps harmonies legible and warm.
